kyoto.name: Kyoto Station 京都駅
kyoto.intro: Better news than you'd expect — but mind the clock: the fast wins are konbini rice + omiyage near the gates; the sit-down GF/vegan gems (even a gluten-free vegan ramen) are up on the restaurant floors and need a transfer with time to spare.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: On a short connection, don't climb to the restaurant floors — these are fast and close to the tracks.
sections.where: Konbini & kiosks on the concourse; JR Isetan B2 depachika (a bit further); omiyage shops by the Central Gate.

rows.t: Konbini: plain salt onigiri, boiled egg, edamame, fruit
rows.note: the universal celiac/vegan train move — read labels; plain salt onigiri is safest
rice/bean based, allergen-labeled; see Omiyage below

rows.t: JR Isetan B2 depachika veg/salad packs
rows.note: undressed vegetable packs; check dressing/dashi

sections.tip: Under ~15 min to change trains: konbini salt-onigiri + edamame + a yatsuhashi from the nearest omiyage stand, and eat on board.

sections.verdict: Real in-station GF/vegan exists — anchored by UNO RAMEN, a dedicated GF+vegan shop; two more vegan-friendly spots were missed by Vegewel; celiacs should still confirm on-site.
sections.where: Kyoto Ramen Koji (10F), JR Isetan 'Eat Paradise' (11F), Porta underground (B1, closer/faster).

rows.note: Dedicated GF+vegan — rice/kombu noodles, GF soy, soymilk broth; kitchen bans gluten & animal products (own site + HappyCow + FindMeGlutenFree); brand-new Dec 2025 so confirm on-site.
Vegan veg/kombu-broth ramen bowl (HappyCow + MATCHA), but wheat noodles + shared fish/meat kitchen — not GF; confirm the veg broth as some variants use bonito-kelp.
Two plant-based donburi confirmed no animal products (Hachidory/Vegewel), but likely wheat soy sauce so GF is ask — confirm on-site.
Own menu shows fish dashi + wheat throughout; vegan set only ‘on advance request’ (Vegewel flags info unverified) — call ahead; not the Kitano flagship.
Udon = wheat; soba usually wheat-blended and broth is kombu + katsuobushi (bonito) dashi — a celiac and vegan trap (kyotostation.com + Tabelog).

sections.tip: For a celiac OR vegan with time, UNO RAMEN is the move — a genuinely GF + vegan ramen inside the station.

sections.verdict: No certified-gluten-free and no truly vegan ekiben here. Almost everything is soy-glazed, fried, or fish/meat/egg based.
sections.where: Inside Shinkansen gates: Grand Kiosk, Delica Station, Maiko, Koto Miyabi (~6:30–21:30). Outside: Ekiben Nigiwai Kyoto (~7:00).

rows.note: soy-glazed beef and egg
wheat batter + soy
sweet soy, dashi, egg
least-bad GF candidate — vinegar-cured fish + rice + kombu — but cure/seasoning can carry wheat; read the label
closest to vegan, but pickles often use soy or bonito dashi; verify

sections.tip: If you must grab a bento, vinegar-cured saba-zushi is the least-bad GF gamble — read the label; nothing here is celiac-safe.

sections.verdict: Packaged = allergen-labeled, so you can verify 小麦 (wheat). And Kyoto's #1 souvenir, yatsuhashi, is rice-based — a real GF/vegan win.
sections.where: The CUBE (Omiyage Koji Kyomachi 1F; 2F Omiyage Kaidō, Nishitoshi pickles, Ogura wagashi), Omiyage Kaidō (Porta), Kyoto Porta, JR Isetan depachika. ~6:00–23:45.

rows.note: rice flour + sugar + cinnamon; most anko/nikki are vegan. Wheat-free recipe BUT shared-wheat facilities (cross-contamination) — fine for gluten-avoiders, celiacs weigh that line. Aoyama Mameju is labeled vegan+GF; Nishio & Shōgoin run 米粉 lines.
rice / bean / agar based; watch honey
many just salt & vinegar; some use soy or bonito dashi — read the label
rice but usually soy-glazed (wheat); shio/salt ones may be OK
wheat (and often egg/dairy)
naturally; check flavored versions

sections.tip: Hero buy: nama-yatsuhashi (anko/nikki) — vegan and rice-based, grabbable in seconds.

playbook.tight: Under ~15 min to change trains: konbini plain salt onigiri (塩むすび) + edamame + a yatsuhashi from the nearest omiyage stand — all near the gates, eat on board.
playbook.gf: Real meal with time: UNO RAMEN (GF+vegan, 10F). Quick: konbini salt onigiri, boiled egg, edamame, fruit + omiyage rice-flour yatsuhashi/yokan. Skip ekiben. Nearest DEDICATED GF bento is Michinori in Nagoya.
playbook.vegan: Real meal with time: UNO RAMEN (10F) or Kyo-Tofu Fujino (11F). Quick: Isetan B2 veg packs + konbini salt onigiri & edamame + omiyage (yatsuhashi, yokan). Off-station: Gen, Hachidori Table. Always ask 'dashi wa sakana?'

hiroshima.name: Hiroshima Station 広島駅
hiroshima.intro: Hiroshima Station is a busy Shinkansen-to-tram hub with the large ekie complex attached, but it has no verified dedicated GF or vegan sit-down restaurant inside the gates — for a tight celiac/vegan transfer your safest bets are konbini staples and a few carefully-chosen omiyage. Read every label: wheat soy sauce and bonito dashi hide in almost all the local specialities.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: Doable — the Seven-Eleven Heart-in is inside the gates (Shinkansen and local-line sides), so plain rice, fruit and soymilk are minutes away; assemble your own meal rather than trusting prepared items.
sections.where: Seven-Eleven Heart-in inside the ticket gates (2F, across from the central gate, and on the local-line side); konbini and depachika in ekie 1F just outside the gates.

rows.t: Shio-musubi 塩むすび (plain salt rice ball)
rows.note: Plain salt and rice is the simplest pick; avoid filled onigiri — most use wheat-containing soy sauce or bonito, so confirm it is salt-only.
Pickled-plum rice is usually plant-based, but seasoning/shiso mixes vary and some add dashi — check the label.
Whole or pre-cut fruit is the most reliable GF and vegan grab; pure fruit with no syrup or seasoning.
Plain roasted unsalted nuts are fine; flavoured or coated nuts can carry wheat or soy seasoning, so check.
Adjusted (調整) versions add flavourings; the unsweetened 無調整 carton is plain soybeans and water.
Vegan-certified flavours exist, but the bars contain wheat-based ingredients — vegan yes, not GF.

sections.tip: Build a tray from single-ingredient items (rice, fruit, nuts, soymilk) instead of relying on a labelled bento — it is the fastest safe option when the clock is tight.

sections.verdict: No verified GF/vegan WALK-IN sit-down inside ekie — but Vegewel missed OKOSTA, an in-ekie okonomiyaki cooking experience with genuine gluten-free and vegetarian courses (reservation-only class, not a restaurant).
sections.where: ekie 6F restaurant floor and ekie 1F dining are mostly conventional Japanese eateries; for verified vegan dining you must leave the station.

rows.t: OKOSTA オコスタ (in ekie, near Shinkansen exit)
rows.note: GF course uses rice-flour noodles + GF-compatible sauce; a vegetarian course exists but batter has egg & dashi may be used; shared teppan with wheat okonomiyaki, no published cross-contamination protocol — celiacs treat as ask (Otafuku own site + gurutabi + dive-hiroshima + Food Diversity).
Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki = wheat batter + wheat noodles + gluten sauce + bonito/pork dashi + shared griddle — no GF/vegan menu found.
Wheat noodles + pork/wheat-soy broth.
Farm-to-table local produce but no published GF/vegan menu — would have to ask on-site.
Genuine vegan + GF menus but NOT in-station — ~1.5–2 km near Hatchobori despite Vegewel filing it under ‘Hiroshima Station’; confirm hours (closed Mon/Tue).

sections.tip: If you have a real layover, eat before you reach the gates or pre-book an off-station vegan cafe — do not count on an in-station dedicated GF/vegan kitchen, because none is confirmed.

sections.verdict: Hiroshima's famous ekiben are built on the exact traps a celiac/vegan must avoid — anago (eel) and oyster bento use wheat soy sauce, sweet tare and bonito, and are animal-based. Treat them as a no by default.
sections.where: Ekiben counters on the Shinkansen concourse and in ekie (Hiroshima Ekiben Co. lineup).

rows.note: Eel is not vegan, and the rice/tare is cooked with wheat-containing soy sauce — not GF either.
Seasonal oyster bento — oysters are not vegan, and rice cooked in oyster stock plus fried oysters (wheat batter) and soy seasoning rule out GF.
All-oyster bento with fried oyster (wheat batter) and yuzu-miso; shellfish and wheat — avoid.
A plain-rice bento is the only conceivable fallback, but garnishes, pickles and furikake often carry soy/dashi — inspect every component.

sections.tip: Hiroshima ekiben are essentially off-limits for strict GF/vegan; default to a konbini shio-musubi plus fruit instead of risking the speciality bento.

sections.verdict: The big win is Nishikido (にしき堂, ekie 2F): its rice-flour nama-momiji is wheat-free, and its Momiji Manju Light is made with no wheat, egg or dairy — but both are made in a shared wheat facility, so it is 'ask' for strict celiacs. Setouchi lemon condiments are the cleanest GF+vegan gifts.
sections.where: Nishikido 1-gō-ten, ekie 2F (approx 8:00–21:00); ekie omiyage zone; Setouchi/Hiroshima brand counters.

rows.note: Mochi/rice-flour skin with no wheat in the recipe, but it contains egg and is made on shared wheat lines — not vegan (egg), GF only if cross-contamination is acceptable, so ask.

rows.t: Momiji Manju Light もみじ饅頭Light (Nishikido)
rows.note: Made free of the 7 major allergens (no wheat, egg or dairy — uses rice flour and soymilk), so vegan-friendly; but the factory also handles wheat/egg/dairy, so end GF on ask for celiacs.
Standard momiji-manju and Setouchi lemon cakes use a wheat-flour sponge, plus egg/dairy — avoid on both counts.
Lemon juice, salted chilli, brewed vinegar, salt and lemon peel — no allergens listed and plant-based; a reliable GF+vegan Hiroshima gift.
Additive-free lemon syrups (lemon + beet sugar) are GF and vegan, but some candies/jams add gelatin or honey — check each product.
Hiroshima oyster gifts are shellfish-based (not vegan) and oyster sauce/seasonings typically include wheat — skip.

sections.tip: Go straight to Nishikido on ekie 2F for the rice-flour momiji items, and grab a bottle of Lemoscco — between them you cover a memorable GF/vegan Hiroshima gift, just flag the shared-facility caveat to a strict celiac.

playbook.tight: Hit the Seven-Eleven Heart-in inside the gates and build a meal from shio-musubi, whole fruit, plain nuts and unsweetened soymilk — fastest reliable option with no kitchen to quiz.
playbook.gf: Assume every Hiroshima speciality (okonomiyaki, ramen, anago/oyster ekiben) carries wheat soy sauce, bonito dashi or wheat batter; lean on plain rice, fruit and Lemoscco, and treat Nishikido's rice-flour momiji as 'ask' because of shared wheat lines.
playbook.vegan: Watch for hidden bonito dashi in 'vegetable' dishes and onigiri seasonings; eel and oysters are never vegan; safe vegan picks are plain rice, fruit, unsweetened soymilk, Lemoscco and Nishikido Momiji Manju Light, with off-station Vegewel cafes for a proper sit-down meal.

kanazawa.name: Kanazawa Station 金沢駅
kanazawa.intro: Kanazawa is a seafood-and-wagashi town, so the station leans fish-heavy (nodoguro, crab) and wheat-rich (Kaga fu, kintsuba) — plan around the clock. If your transfer is tight, grab konbini staples by the gates; with time to spare, the Hyakubangai shops reward a slower look.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: Konbini are your reliable fallback: there is a Seven-Eleven Kiosk on the Shinkansen platform mezzanine (inside the gates) plus larger Heart-in stores around the concourse. Nothing is celiac-certified, so read labels and treat everything as cross-contamination risk.
sections.where: Seven-Eleven Kiosk JR Kanazawa platform mezzanine (inside gates); Seven-Eleven Heart-in by the concourse; Hyakubangai food halls.

rows.note: Plainest rice balls are the safest bet; skip fillings with mayo, tuna, or soy-seasoned mix — and ask, as seasonings can hide wheat.
Whole or pre-cut fruit is the no-think option when in doubt.
Seasonal konbini staple; just sweet potato, naturally GF and vegan.
Plain veg is fine but bagged dressings often use wheat soy sauce; take it undressed or check the packet — ask.
Unseasoned nuts and salted edamame travel well; avoid soy-glazed snack mixes.

sections.tip: Carry your own GF tamari/snacks — the station has no celiac-certified hot food, so build a meal from naturally GF konbini parts rather than relying on a single product.

sections.verdict: Verified GF/vegan sit-down INSIDE the station is essentially nil — the official Hyakubangai directory shows no dedicated GF or vegan restaurant; Onyasai shabu-shabu is the only realistically negotiable ‘ask’ fallback.
sections.where: Kanazawa Hyakubangai あんと / あんと西 restaurant zone (connected to the gates); Ume no Hana (reserve ahead).

rows.note: Customizable veg/tofu plates make it the best in-station fallback, but ponzu/sesame sauces & broths often carry wheat soy + bonito dashi — confirm broth and sauces.

rows.t: Ume no Hana 梅の花 (tofu/yuba kaiseki) — NOT in-station
rows.note: Vegan-on-reservation is real (own site), but it is NOT in-station — own site + Tabelog place it ~2.4 km / 8-min drive away; Vegewel’s ‘station’ tag is proximity only.
Whole concept is 麩 (wheat gluten) — not GF; confirmed via Fumuroya’s own site + Hyakubangai listing.
Veg ramen is veg-forward but allergen label confirms 小麦 (wheat noodles) and broth typically has pork/chicken/dashi — not GF, not reliably vegan.
Kanazawa curry roux is wheat-flour-thickened (allergen label confirms 小麦) over a meat/lard base — a celiac and vegan trap.

sections.tip: If you have a fixed Kanazawa stop, reserve Ume no Hana a day ahead and state celiac + vegan clearly; for a transfer, a sushi counter with your own GF tamari is the most flexible option.

sections.verdict: Kanazawa ekiben are a seafood showcase (nodoguro, snow crab, Noto beef) sold by historic makers like Takano Shoten, Daimaruya/Otomorou and Funayoshi — delicious but built on wheat soy sauce, mirin and bonito dashi. None are celiac-safe; vegans will struggle as nearly all are fish or meat.
sections.where: Ekiben stands inside the gates and in Hyakubangai あんと.

rows.note: Fish simmered/grilled in soy-mirin seasoning over dashi rice — wheat soy sauce and bonito throughout.
Crab itself is GF but vinegared rice and accompanying soy sauce usually contain wheat — ask, and it is never vegan.
Sampler box leans on soy-mirin glazes and dashi; multiple wheat/animal traps in one bento.
Jibuni thickens duck in wheat-soy dashi broth — classic wheat trap, not vegan.

sections.tip: Treat Kanazawa ekiben as a no-go for strict celiac; if you must eat one, plain rice-and-grilled-fish layers are the least risky but still assume wheat soy sauce — ask the vendor.

sections.verdict: Kanazawa is a wagashi capital, which is good news: many sweets are rice/bean/agar-based and naturally GF + vegan. The traps are the wheat ones (kintsuba coating, manju, monaka, castella, and Kaga fu) and any bonito-laced fermented fish like kabura-zushi. Check each box; cross-contamination is possible in shared kitchens.
sections.where: Hyakubangai あんと omiyage zone — Moroeya, Nakataya, Morihachi, Fumuroya, plus gold-leaf shops.

rows.note: Pressed mochi-rice flour and wasanbon sugar — naturally GF and vegan; a reliable Kanazawa gift.
Just mochi rice and azuki bean paste — GF and vegan; short shelf life, so eat soon.
Bean filling is plant-based but the signature thin coating is wheat flour — not GF for celiac.
Edible gold leaf is inert; check the base — agar/bean/rice versions can be GF+vegan, but castella or wheat bases are not, and gold-dusted senbei may use soy — ask.

rows.t: Kaga-bocha (roasted stem tea) 加賀棒茶
rows.note: Plain roasted tea leaves — GF and vegan; an easy, safe gift.
Fermented with yellowtail and rice-koji, often in wheat-soy seasoning — fish-based and not GF or vegan.

sections.tip: Lean on rakugan, ankoro mochi and kaga-bocha for trustworthy gifts; for kintsuba, monaka, manju, castella and Kaga fu assume wheat, and skip kabura-zushi entirely if vegan.

playbook.tight: On a <15-min connection, head straight to the Seven-Eleven Kiosk on the Shinkansen platform mezzanine (inside the gates) and build a plate from plain onigiri, cut fruit and edamame — don't gamble on a hot or seasoned item you can't read in time.
playbook.gf: Nothing in-station is celiac-certified; carry your own GF tamari, default to plain rice/fruit/nuts and naturally-GF wagashi (rakugan, ankoro mochi), and treat soy sauce, dashi, kintsuba coating and Kaga fu as wheat. When unsure, ask.
playbook.vegan: Watch hidden bonito dashi in oden, ekiben and sushi-rice seasonings; safest picks are plain konbini staples and rice/bean/agar wagashi, and for a real meal reserve Ume no Hana ahead and state vegan clearly.

nagano.name: Nagano Station 長野駅
nagano.intro: Nagano is a soba town, and soba here is the trap rather than the saviour — almost all of it is wheat-cut and the tsuyu hangs on bonito dashi. For a tight Shinkansen transfer, lean on fruit packs, apples and naturally-GF/vegan omiyage; a verified in-station sit-down meal that is both GF and vegan is hard to pin down, so plan ahead.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: Best bet for a tight connection: whole fruit and packaged snacks. Hot food at the konbini is wheat-and-dashi territory.
sections.where: NewDays / NewDays MINI / KIOSK inside the Shinkansen gates; Shinano-no-Kaze and Shina-no-ki kiosks at the Shinkansen gate (approx 6:30–19:30); MIDORI depachika one floor down for fruit and veg packs.

rows.note: Nagano's signature — whole or pre-cut fruit is the cleanest GF+vegan option in the building.
Rice itself is fine, but most fillings carry wheat soy sauce or bonito; a plain shio onigiri is the closest — read the label or ask.
Plain fruit/veg is fine; skip any with dressing, custard or a mayo-based dip.
Walnuts and Nagano are a pair, but flavoured/coated snacks often add wheat, malt or dairy — check the ingredient line.
Unsweetened tea and 100% apple juice are easy wins for a quick hydration grab.

sections.tip: If the clock is against you, a bag of apples plus bottled tea is the fail-safe — don't gamble on a hot konbini item you can't verify.

sections.verdict: Confirmed — nothing verified as BOTH gluten-free AND vegan sit-down inside MIDORI Nagano; GF-capable and vegan options exist on separate axes, and the best vegan spot is a short walk from the Zenkō-ji exit, not in the building.
sections.where: MIDORI 長野 station building, 3F 'Ogosso Dining' restaurant floor (soba); Yamano-Ma Brewery is ~2 min from the Zenkō-ji exit; Zenkō-ji shukubō (temple lodgings) ~30 min walk / short bus.

rows.t: Shinshu Jūwari Soba Ten 信州十割そば てん (station front)
rows.note: 十割 = 100% buckwheat with GF tempura (buckwheat+rice flour, rice oil) and no wheat dusting — the closest station GF find — but tsuyu = bonito dashi + wheat soy, so not vegan and ask for GF tamari; at station front, likely not inside MIDORI proper.
Own site states 七割 (70% buckwheat = 30% wheat) so not GF; tempura = wheat, tsuyu = dashi + wheat soy (Kusabue official + MIDORI floor guide).

rows.t: そば処 みよ田 Miyota (MIDORI 3F)
rows.note: Mill-owned; ratio unpublished (has wheat) and dashi = kombu + bonito → not vegan, wheat soy in tsuyu → not GF (Nikkoku official + nagano-cvb).

rows.t: Yamano-Ma Brewery 山の間 (Zenkō-ji exit) — NOT in-station
rows.note: Clear star-marked vegan menu (tofu burger, spring rolls, veg hot pot), but craft beer is NOT GF, it is ~15:00–21:00 closed Tue–Wed, and a ~2-min walk — not in-station (HappyCow + Vegewel).

sections.tip: Only Nagano soba made juwari (十割, no wheat) with a wheat-free tsuyu and no shared boiling water would be GF — that combination is not verified in-station, so treat station soba as off-limits for celiac and ask in person if tempted.

sections.verdict: Nagano's ekiben are meat-and-fish heavy (beef, salmon, pork) and seasoned with wheat soy sauce; most are neither GF nor vegan. Treat as a last resort and read labels.
sections.where: Shinano-no-Kaze and Shina-no-ki at the Shinkansen gate; Meiji-tei near the MIDORI entrance; in-gate ekiben stands.

rows.note: Wheat soy sauce in the sukiyaki sauce and beef throughout — out for both diets.
Pork, miso sauce and roasted soba grains over rice — wheat in the seasoning and not plant-based.
Oyaki is a WHEAT-flour dumpling — never GF; veg-filled ones may be vegan but check for dashi/egg in the fillings.
Mixed-box bento with fish, egg and soy-seasoned sides — wheat soy and bonito throughout.

sections.tip: Ekiben labels list allergens (小麦=wheat) by law — scan that line before buying, and assume any sauce-glazed item has wheat soy sauce.

sections.verdict: Best gifts for restricted diets are apple products and agar-set Misuzu-ame; soba goods and oyaki are wheat traps despite the 'soba' name.
sections.where: MIDORI 'Shinshu Omiyage Sandō ORAHO' (1F/gate area) and station gift kiosks.

rows.note: The flagship is fruit juice + sugar + agar (kanten) — vegan and GF; but some specialty jellies add gelatin, so check '寒天 only, no ゼラチン' on that product.

rows.t: Apple products — dried apple, 100% juice, jam
rows.note: Plain dried apple/juice/jam is GF+vegan; baked apple sweets (ringo cakes) contain wheat and often dairy — those are 'no'.
Shinshu miso is rice-koji miso (rice + soybean + salt), usually wheat-free and plant-based — but confirm 'komugi' isn't listed and that it's not a dashi-blended miso.
Only genuine juwari with NO wheat is GF, and it must be cooked in clean water — most 'Shinshu soba' gifts are wheat-cut (nihachi); read for 小麦 and treat as 'ask'.
Kurumi confections are a Nagano signature but built on wheat cookie/monaka and often butter — generally not GF and frequently not vegan.
Wheat-dough dumpling — never GF; some veg fillings may be vegan but check for dashi and lard.

sections.tip: For a giftable that clears both diets, pair agar-set Misuzu-ame with plain apple juice or dried apple — and always scan the 小麦/ゼラチン allergen line.

playbook.tight: Skip the soba and the konbini hot case — grab Nagano apples or a cut-fruit pack plus bottled tea from the in-gate kiosks/depachika and run for your train.
playbook.gf: Assume all station soba is wheat-cut and every tsuyu/sauce has wheat soy sauce; safest GF buys are whole apples, plain apple products and agar Misuzu-ame. For any soba or bento, read the 小麦 allergen line and, if uncertain, ask — never assume.
playbook.vegan: Watch for hidden bonito dashi in every soba tsuyu and bento; the only verified in-area vegan sit-down is Yamano-Ma Brewery (Zenkō-ji exit, dinner-only) or, with a reservation, Zenkō-ji temple shōjin-ryōri — otherwise build a plant plate from fruit, plain veg and rice.

shin_osaka.name: Shin-Osaka Station 新大阪駅
shin_osaka.intro: Shin-Osaka is a giant Shinkansen-to-local interchange where transfers can be brutally tight, and almost every Osaka specialty here (takoyaki, okonomiyaki, 551 butaman) is wheat. For celiacs, treat this station as a grab-fruit-and-konbini-onigiri stop and save real meals for verified spots; a genuine GF/vegan sit-down restaurant inside the JR station could not be verified.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: Konbini and kiosks are your fastest bet; pick whole, single-ingredient items and read labels, since most prepared foods hide wheat soy sauce and bonito dashi.
sections.where: Seven-Eleven Heart-in (inside the JR local east gate, from 06:30), Bellmart kiosks inside the Shinkansen gates, and Eki Marché エキマルシェ新大阪 takeout stands just outside the local gates.

rows.t: Salted rice onigiri (塩むすび / shio-musubi)
rows.note: Plainest onigiri is rice + salt; avoid tuna-mayo and any with soy-sauce seasoning, and check the label for wheat — ask.
Usually just rice, plum and salt, but seasoned-rice versions can carry dashi or soy — confirm the label, so ask.
Konbini bananas or a fruit cup are the cleanest no-label-needed option for a tight connection.

rows.t: Plain roasted nuts / rice-based snack packs (konbini)
rows.note: Many senbei use wheat-containing soy sauce and shared lines; only buy if the label is wheat-free, otherwise ask.
Iconic here but a wheat-flour bun with pork and soy sauce; not GF and not vegan — skip it.

sections.tip: For celiacs the safest grab is rice + salt + fruit; nothing hot off a counter here is verified gluten-free, so default to sealed, labeled items.

sections.verdict: Confirmed — no verified GF or reliable-vegan sit-down inside the JR Shin-Osaka gates (Eki Marché, Eki Marché West, concourse, Alde); the dedicated vegan+GF spot is one stop away at Osaka Station.
sections.where: Best verified GF+vegan kitchen is at Osaka/Umeda (one stop away); the vegetarian Chinese option near Shin-Osaka is in a hotel by the subway, not in the JR concourse.

rows.t: Inside JR gates (Eki Marché / Alde / concourse)
rows.note: Full Alde floor map (ramen, Botejyu, 551 Horai, sushi, tonkatsu) shows no dedicated GF/vegan venue; ramen = wheat + bonito/pork, sushi uses wheat soy — treat everything in-gate as no / ask.

rows.t: Vegan & Gluten-Free Osaka (Second Room Cafe) — Umeda, 1 stop
rows.note: 100% vegan + GF Osaka soul food (okonomiyaki, takoyaki, kushikatsu) — NOT in Shin-Osaka; it is Umeda, ~3-min walk from JR Osaka Station one stop south (own site + insideosaka).

rows.t: BelleVue Keisyoro (Chinese, veg) — hotel, outside gates
rows.note: In the Shin-Osaka Station Hotel (~2-min walk from Exit 7, outside the gates); wheat soy + shared kitchen so not GF, and the ‘vegetarian menu’ is a Vegewel-only reservation course — confirm.

sections.tip: If you truly need a verified GF+vegan meal, ride one stop to Osaka/Umeda for Vegan & Gluten-Free Osaka rather than gambling inside Shin-Osaka.

sections.verdict: Shin-Osaka has a strong ekiben lineup (Tabiben 駅弁にぎわい), but the standard bento are built on wheat soy sauce and bonito dashi, so they fail celiac and often vegan tests.
sections.where: Tabiben 駅弁にぎわい and bento counters in Eki Marché and on the Shinkansen concourse.

rows.note: Iconic soy-simmered octopus + conger in a takotsubo pot; standard recipe uses wheat soy sauce and wheat takoten, plus octopus — not GF, not vegan.

A real GF version exists (rice-noodle base, whole-soybean wheat-free soy sauce) but it is a frozen/Expo product, not the regular platform ekiben — don't assume it's on the kiosk shelf; ask, and note it still has octopus/egg so not vegan.
Marinades and tare are wheat soy-sauce based and the dish is beef — not GF, not vegan.
Looks plant-friendly but simmered veg use dashi and soy sauce; hidden bonito and wheat are likely — not GF, ask for vegan.

sections.tip: For a celiac, the only ekiben worth pursuing is Awajiya's explicit GF line — and only if a wheat-free label is physically on the box that day; otherwise ekiben here are off-limits.

sections.verdict: Osaka's headline gifts are nearly all wheat (butaman, takoyaki/okonomiyaki snacks, cheese tarts), so hunt deliberately for rice-based or dried items and still check labels.
sections.where: Eki Marché omiyage hall, the 2F/3F gift floors, and Omiyage Kaido / Daily Service souvenir counters.

rows.t: 551 Horai butaman / chimaki — AVOID for GF & vegan
rows.note: Wheat-flour bun, pork and wheat-containing soy sauce; the chimaki also has soy/wheat — neither GF nor vegan.

rows.t: Pablo / cheese tart & Osaka baked sweets
rows.note: Wheat crust plus dairy and egg — not GF, not vegan.
Wheat batter, bonito and often dairy/egg — a classic wheat trap; avoid.
Many list wheat flour and use wheat soy sauce — assume rice crackers here are NOT GF unless the label proves otherwise.
Mochi/azuki sweets can be wheat-free and egg/dairy-free, but some use wheat-starch (wasanbon mixes) or honey — read the label, so ask.
Whole umeboshi or plain nori are clean plant-based gifts; just confirm seasoned versions are free of wheat soy sauce/dashi — ask.

sections.tip: Reframe omiyage as 'rice + dried + sweet-bean' here; the flour-food Osaka is famous for is exactly what a celiac must skip.

playbook.tight: On a tight Shinkansen connection, go straight to Seven-Eleven Heart-in or a Bellmart kiosk and grab a salted/umeboshi onigiri plus fruit — read the onigiri label for wheat, and don't risk anything hot off a counter.
playbook.gf: No in-station GF restaurant is verified; for celiacs the realistic plan is sealed, labeled rice items (plain onigiri, fruit, wheat-free wagashi) at Shin-Osaka, and saving a proper GF meal for Vegan & Gluten-Free Osaka one stop away in Umeda — never assume soy sauce, dashi or ekiben are wheat-free; when unsure, ask.
playbook.vegan: Vegans can do well on plain onigiri, fruit, umeboshi, nori and bean wagashi, but watch for hidden bonito dashi in any cooked/bento item and skip butaman, octopus ekiben and sushi; for a hot vegan meal, ride one stop to Vegan & Gluten-Free Osaka in Umeda.

himeji.name: Himeji Station 姫路駅
himeji.intro: Himeji's whole castle↔station corridor is now a full curated map in this app (switch to the Himeji city) — so for a real meal, use the map. This card is the grab-&-go / ekiben / omiyage layer for a tight connection.

sections.h: Tight transfer? Grab & go near the gates
sections.verdict: Konbini + the Piole depachika are your fast, label-readable options; the famous station eki-soba is wheat, so skip it for GF.
sections.where: Seven-Eleven/kiosks on the concourse; Piole Himeji depachika (fruit, veg packs, wagashi); omiyage stands.

rows.t: Plain salt onigiri, boiled egg, edamame, fruit
rows.note: The universal move — read labels; plain shio onigiri is safest.
Undressed vegetable/fruit packs; check dressing/dashi.

rows.t: Maneki 'eki-soba' 駅そば — AVOID for GF
rows.note: Himeji's iconic yellow Chinese wheat noodles in bonito dashi — wheat + fish; a landmark, but not GF or vegan.

sections.tip: Tight connection: konbini salt onigiri + edamame + a rice-flour omiyage (below), eat on board.

sections.verdict: Use the Himeji CITY MAP in this app — the castle↔station corridor has a full curated set (incl. dedicated vegan and GF-friendly spots). One in-station-area organic café is listed but single-sourced.
sections.where: Ōtemae-dōri (station↔castle) — see the Himeji city map. Piole Himeji restaurant floors.

rows.note: 120 curated corridor spots with honest GF/vegan classification — 4 vegan, 1 GF-dedicated, 1 vegan+GF among them. The real answer for a sit-down meal.
Listed on Vegewel with vegan + GF menus — single-sourced, so confirm hours & GF handling directly before going.

sections.tip: For a genuine GF/vegan meal, browse the Himeji city map rather than the station stalls.

sections.verdict: Maneki has sold Himeji ekiben for 130+ years, but they're soy-glazed eel/beef — not GF, not vegan.
sections.where: Maneki Foods stands on the concourse and platforms.

rows.note: Conger eel simmered in soy tare over dashi rice — wheat soy + eel.
Dashi rice with beef, shrimp, octopus, egg — soy and animal throughout.

sections.tip: Ekiben here are off-limits for strict GF/vegan; default to konbini + omiyage.

sections.verdict: Packaged = allergen-labeled. Nerikiri/bean wagashi and a genuine rice-flour item are the wins; the famous wheat sweets are traps.
sections.where: Piole Himeji, festa, and station omiyage counters (Iseya, Gozasōrō, etc.).

rows.t: 官兵衛 兵糧餅 (Kanbei hyōrō-mochi)
rows.note: Made with rice flour, no wheat (maker states komeko/GF) — a rare rice-based Himeji find; confirm the label & shared-facility caveat.
Nerikiri (bean + rice) wagashi — often wheat-free and plant-based, but read the label (some contain wheat/egg).
Bean filling is plant-based but the pancake batter is wheat — not GF.
Wheat sweets (and dairy/egg in the almond-toast line) — avoid.
Rice/bean/agar based — naturally GF/vegan; check for honey.

sections.tip: Hero buy: the rice-flour 兵糧餅, or plain nerikiri/yokan — read labels.

playbook.tight: Under ~15 min: konbini salt onigiri + edamame + a rice-flour omiyage (官兵衛 兵糧餅) — skip the wheat eki-soba.
playbook.gf: Real meal: open the Himeji city map (curated corridor, incl. GF-friendly spots). Quick: konbini salt onigiri, boiled egg, edamame, fruit + rice-flour 兵糧餅 / yokan. Skip eki-soba and anago ekiben (wheat).
playbook.vegan: Real meal: Himeji city map (4 vegan spots in the corridor). Quick: konbini salt onigiri + edamame + fruit + bean wagashi. Ask 'dashi wa sakana?'; skip eki-soba/ekiben.
